chain_of_thought: Native stack only shows an abort with no symbolized intermediate frames; only identifiable app frame is the JNI entry Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString in libheresdk.so.
Allocator/sanitizer frames are not shown; the top-level is abort, which strongly suggests an internal assertion or explicit abort rather than a wild crash like SEGV or buffer overflow.

We attempted to use Ghidra MCP (search_functions_by_name, list_functions) on libheresdk.so but the backend responded with HTTP 404 errors, so we cannot decompile the JNI function or see its implementation, meaning we cannot trace memcpy/memmove or length arguments as required.

Because of this tooling failure, we cannot perform the mandated detailed backward data-flow analysis from the first app-level frame below allocators; all we know is that the JNI method eventually leads to abort().

Despite this, we can reason at a higher level: the JNI bridge GeoCoordinates_fromString likely parses a string into latitude/longitude.Structurally, realistic bugs here are format parsing issues (e.g., invalid characters, out-of-range coordinates) that usually cause logic errors or explicit validation aborts, not low-level memory corruption.

The termination reason is plain abort, with no mention of SIGSEGV, SIGBUS, stack buffer overflow, or allocator corruption; in many mobile SDKs, invalid input to parsers triggers internal asserts or explicit aborts to signal programmer misuse.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input almost certainly feeds arbitrary data into this string-based API; fuzzers routinely hit assertion failures and defensive aborts that are not exploitable vulnerabilities, especially when invariants normally guaranteed by app logic or higher-level APIs are violated.

We have no evidence of unsafe primitives (memcpy with attacker-controlled length, pointer arithmetic, etc.) nor any indication of memory corruption; just an abort, so classifying this as a memory-safety vulnerability would be speculation.

Given that we cannot inspect code and the observable behavior is a simple abort likely due to failed precondition or assertion, the more defensible stance is that this is a harness/unrealistic-input issue or at most a non-exploitable DoS when misused, not a security vulnerability per the definition (no clear attacker-controlled unsafe behavior).

Therefore, we label this crash as not a confirmed vulnerability, with moderate-to-low confidence because deeper code inspection is blocked by tooling, and we recommend obtaining full symbols and re-running analysis if stronger evidence appears (e.g., SEGVs in parsing code).
reasons: Crash reason is a plain abort() with no indication of memory corruption or control-flow hijack.
Only identifiable native frame is the JNI entry GeoCoordinates_fromString; the most likely failure is an internal assert or validation abort on malformed coordinate strings supplied by the fuzzer.
Tooling errors (404 from Ghidra MCP) prevent decompiling libheresdk.so, so there is no concrete evidence of unsafe memory operations tied to attacker-controlled input.

Fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input may be calling the JNI API with unrealistic parameters or invariants that normal app code would never violate, making this more likely an environmental/precondition failure.
recommendations: Re-run analysis with full symbols and working Ghidra MCP to inspect Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString implementation for unsafe memory operations.
Augment fuzzing harness to better approximate realistic usage (valid UTF-8, reasonable length limits, coordinate-like formats) to avoid predominantly hitting assertion-style aborts.
If available, capture logs or enable debug builds of libheresdk.so to see assertion messages or error codes leading to abort().
assumptions: GeoCoordinates_fromString takes a string-like argument derived from fuzzer input.
The abort is triggered by internal validation or an assertion rather than by unhandled memory corruption.
No additional sanitizer output (ASan, UBSan) is present beyond the provided stack trace.
limitations: Unable to decompile or disassemble functions in libheresdk.so due to MCP HTTP 404 errors.
No Java callgraph details beyond the single JNI bridge method, so argument provenance and validation on the Java side are unknown.
Stack trace lacks intermediate frames between JNI and abort, preventing more precise localization of failing code.
